QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY NIHAD ALIYEV AND XINYI DENG University of Technology Sydney Abstract. We study the impact of quantitative trading strategies (quants) on price efficiency by developing a model that incorporates two defining features of quants: superior information processing capacity and limited strategic awareness. These forces have opposing effects on trading aggressiveness—better information pro- cessing increases it, while limited strategic awareness decreases it. As a result, an increase in quant participation deteriorates price efficiency when their information quality is low, improves it when information quality is high, and produces a non-monotonic effect in between. JEL Classification: D40, D82, G14, G20 Keywords: Investment management, quantitative trading, market efficiency Date: April 26, 2025. (∗) Nihad Aliyev (nihad.aliyev@uts.edu.au) and Xinyi Deng (xinyi.deng@student.uts.edu.au). 1 2 1. Introduction Advances in artificial intelligence (AI), along with the av

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: iciency Date: April 26, 2025. (∗) Nihad Aliyev (nihad.aliyev@uts.edu.au) and Xinyi Deng (xinyi.deng@student.uts.edu.au). 1 2 1. Introduction Advances in artificial intelligence (AI), along with the availability of big data and com- puting power, have changed how investment decisions are made. These developments have enabled a new generation of investors, commonly known asquants, who use quanti- tative models and algorithms to identify trading opportunities, construct portfolios, and execute trades at scale. While quantitative investing was once limited to a niche subset of hedge funds, it has become increasingly mainstream over the past two decades. Today, it is widely accessible, even to retail investors, through quantitative trading platforms and mutual funds (e.g., Beggs and Hill-Kleespie 2025). As quants take up a larger share of trading activity, concerns have emerged about how their collective behavior might affect market stability.1 Episodes of extreme price disloca- tions such

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: ie 2025). As quants take up a larger share of trading activity, concerns have emerged about how their collective behavior might affect market stability.1 Episodes of extreme price disloca- tions such as the August 2007 Quant Meltdown and the May 2010 Flash Crash have raised questions about whether these strategies can, at times, contribute to market stress. While they are often praised for their discipline and ability to eliminate behavioral biases, an emerging body of evidence suggests that quants trading on similar signals and models may react simultaneously to the same events, resulting in crowded trades, fire sales, and feedback loops that can destabilize markets (e.g., Khandani and Lo 2011; Cai et al. 2019; Falato et al. 2021). The growing presence of quants raises important questions about how they influence other investors and the functioning of markets. Unlike traditional discretionary investors who rely on human judgment, quants tend to process information differently and trad

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: about how they influence other investors and the functioning of markets. Unlike traditional discretionary investors who rely on human judgment, quants tend to process information differently and trade based on systematic strategies. As more quants enter the market, the strategic environ- ment in which prices are formed changes. Yet we know little about how differences in in- formation processing and strategic awareness between quants and discretionary investors influence markets. This paper addresses two key questions: What strategic forces influ- ence investor behavior and price formation as quants and discretionary investors compete? And how does the growing participation of quants impact market efficiency? 1 According to the Tabb Group, quantitative hedge funds accounted for 31% of all U.S. stock trades in 2019. See, ”The Making of the World’s Greatest Investor,” The Wall Street Journal, November 2, 2019. 3 To address these questions, we develop a theoretical model in the tradition

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: U.S. stock trades in 2019. See, ”The Making of the World’s Greatest Investor,” The Wall Street Journal, November 2, 2019. 3 To address these questions, we develop a theoretical model in the tradition of Kyle (1985) that incorporates both quants and discretionary investors. The model captures key differences between these two trader types. While quants possess superior informa- tion processing capabilities, they lack the strategic sophistication of discretionary investors (e.g., Abis 2022; Kirilenko and Lo 2013). This reflects a common feature of many algorith- mic strategies, which are often built to exploit signal-driven patterns without accounting for second-order strategic feedback. In the model, there are two investment firms (firm A and B), noise traders, and com- petitive market makers. In the baseline model, we assume firm A is fully discretionary, and firm B consists of a quant and a discretionary department. Before trading begins, nature determines whether the quant or discre

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: ers. In the baseline model, we assume firm A is fully discretionary, and firm B consists of a quant and a discretionary department. Before trading begins, nature determines whether the quant or discretionary department of firmB is active. The probability that the quant department is active captures the quantitative investing level in the economy. The quant department has superior information processing capacity: it observes both the public signal v1 and a noisy signal θ2 about a second component of the asset’s value, v = v1 + v2. In contrast, the discretionary department observes only v1. Despite the in- formational advantage, the quant is strategically weaker. It optimises trades based on signals but incorrectly perceives that firmA does not adjust its strategy in response, i.e., it is strategically myopic. In the baseline model, we assume complete strategic myopia, but later relax this assumption to allow for partial responsiveness. We show that the strategic interactions between qua

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: rategically myopic. In the baseline model, we assume complete strategic myopia, but later relax this assumption to allow for partial responsiveness. We show that the strategic interactions between quants and discretionary investors give rise to three key effects. First, as the quantitative investing level increases, firmB becomes more effective at extracting information from the additional signal θ2 and increases its trading aggressiveness on it, which we refer to as the processing capacity effect. Second, firm B reduces its aggressiveness on v1 due to its inability to account for how its trades influence firm A’s behavior—this reflects the strategic myopia of quants. Third, the fully discretionary firm A responds by trading more aggressively on the public signal v1 to exploit firm B’s strategic weakness, which we term theinternalising effect. 4 The net effect on overall trading aggressiveness depends on the relative strength of these effects and varies with quants’ additional signal q

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: ategic weakness, which we term theinternalising effect. 4 The net effect on overall trading aggressiveness depends on the relative strength of these effects and varies with quants’ additional signal quality (q) and quantitative investing level (γ). We show that the processing capacity effect increases linearly with q. Furthermore, the strategic myopia and internalising effects are both independent of q but vary with γ. As γ increases, the myopia effect decays faster than the internalising effect, implying the greatest reduction in trading aggressiveness at low levels ofγ. These mechanisms have non-monotonic effects on overall trading aggressiveness of traders. When q is low, the strategic myopia effect dominates at all levels of γ, leading to a decrease in overall aggressiveness. Whenq is high, the positive effects dominate and trading aggressiveness increases withγ. At intermediate values ofq, the relationship is U- shaped: overall aggressiveness first decreases, and then increases. T

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: high, the positive effects dominate and trading aggressiveness increases withγ. At intermediate values ofq, the relationship is U- shaped: overall aggressiveness first decreases, and then increases. This result shows that the impact of growing quant participation in markets hinges on both their informational advantage and their ability to account for strategic feedback. Since prices are determined by order flow in our model, changes in trading behavior naturally affect price efficiency. Following Kyle (1985) and related work (e.g., Subrah- manyam 1991; Holden and Subrahmanyam 1992), we define price efficiency as the inverse of the conditional variance of the asset’s value given the price. We show that, similar to trading aggressiveness, when q is low, increasing γ always harms price efficiency. When q is high, it always improves it. At moderate q, the relationship is again U-shaped: price efficiency first decreases, and then increases once the quantitative investing levelγ exceeds a th

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: ency. When q is high, it always improves it. At moderate q, the relationship is again U-shaped: price efficiency first decreases, and then increases once the quantitative investing levelγ exceeds a threshold. This U-shaped pattern implies that whether quants improve or harm price efficiency depends not just on how much information they process, but also on how well they account for strategic feedback. We extend the model in two directions. First, we consider a model where both firms have a quant and a discretionary department. This introduces a fourth effect, which we term the competition effect. As the quantitative investing level of firm B increases, it competes more aggressively on the additional signalθ2, leading firmA’s quant department to reduce its aggressiveness on θ2. Therefore, the impact of increasing quantitative investing of one firm depends critically on the composition of the other firm. 5 In the special case when firmA is already fully quant, increasing the quantitative

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: he impact of increasing quantitative investing of one firm depends critically on the composition of the other firm. 5 In the special case when firmA is already fully quant, increasing the quantitative invest- ing level of firm B still improves its ability to extract information fromθ2, but this comes with more strategic myopia and more competition for the signal. Moreover, because firm A no longer has a discretionary department, it cannot respond strategically to exploit firm B’s myopia, weakening the internalising effect. Under these conditions, we show that an increase in firm B’s quantitative investing level harms price efficiency, even when the sig- nal quality is high. This suggests that the more quant-driven the rest of the market is, the more likely it is that further increases in quantitative investing by an individual firm harm price efficiency. Second, we relax the assumption of complete strategic myopia in the baseline model by allowing firm B’s quant department to be partia

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: uantitative investing by an individual firm harm price efficiency. Second, we relax the assumption of complete strategic myopia in the baseline model by allowing firm B’s quant department to be partially myopic, parameterised byh ∈ (0, 1] which represents the degree of strategic myopia;h = 1 corresponds to the baseline (fully myopic) model andh = 0 corresponds to a fully strategic quant. Ash decreases, both the strategic myopia and internalising effects weaken and vanish as h approaches zero, but the processing capacity effect is independent ofh. Consequently, the impact of increasing the quantitative investing levelγ on trading aggressiveness and price efficiency increases when h is low orq is high, and decreases whenh is high orq is low. Therefore, the direction of the effect is driven by the ratioq/h. 1.1. Related literature. Our paper contributes to the emerging literature on the interac- tion between machines and humans in financial markets (e.g., Abis 2022; Malikov and Pasquariel

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: y the ratioq/h. 1.1. Related literature. Our paper contributes to the emerging literature on the interac- tion between machines and humans in financial markets (e.g., Abis 2022; Malikov and Pasquariello 2025; Beggs and Hill-Kleespie 2025; Barbopoulos et al. 2024; Coleman, Merkley and Pacelli 2022; Cao et al. 2024). We develop a model that isolates four effects arising from the strategic interaction between machine-based quants and human discre- tionaries: the processing capacity effect, driven by quants’ superior ability to extract infor- mation; the strategic myopia effect, reflecting their limited awareness of how their trades influence others; the internalising effect, capturing the strategic response of discretionary investors to quants; and thecompetition effect, which emerges when multiple quants com- pete for the same signals. 6 Our analysis builds on and complements recent work by Abis (2022) and Malikov and Pasquariello (2025). Abis (2022) documents that quantitative and discr

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: ltiple quants com- pete for the same signals. 6 Our analysis builds on and complements recent work by Abis (2022) and Malikov and Pasquariello (2025). Abis (2022) documents that quantitative and discretionary mutual funds differ in their investment styles. Quants focus on stock picking and process more information, while discretionaries have superior macro-timing ability and portfolio flex- ibility. This reflects a trade-off between processing capacity and strategic flexibility. Our model formalizes this trade-off in a strategic setting by showing how quants’ information advantage can be offset by their inability to anticipate and respond to other investors’ be- havior. Malikov and Pasquariello (2025) also develop a model of strategic trading, where quants differ from discretionaries by sticking to backtested strategies. They show that quants can help or hurt market quality, depending on how aggressively they trade. Our model takes a similar approach but differs in two key ways. First,

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: icking to backtested strategies. They show that quants can help or hurt market quality, depending on how aggressively they trade. Our model takes a similar approach but differs in two key ways. First, we model strategic myopia not as adherence to a fixed rule, but as an incorrect belief about the opponent’s strategy, which allows quants to choose profit-maximising responses given their misper- ceptions. Second, we introduce a richer setting with two signals and random assignment between quant and discretionary traders, which lets us isolate how different effects impact market outcomes. Empirically, Beggs and Hill-Kleespie (2025) find that fire sales by quantitative mutual funds have a far greater impact on market instability than those by discretionary funds, largely due to crowded positions and similar trading behavior. Our model helps ratio- nalize this result by showing how strategic myopia leads quants to trade aggressively on signals without accounting for the broader strategic ef

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: ns and similar trading behavior. Our model helps ratio- nalize this result by showing how strategic myopia leads quants to trade aggressively on signals without accounting for the broader strategic effects, which invites exploitation by discretionary investors and amplify price distortions under stress. More broadly, our paper contributes to the literature on the effects of automation in fi- nancial markets (e.g., Hendershott, Jones and Menkveld 2011; Weller 2018; Dugast and Foucault 2018; Farboodi et al. 2022). We add to this literature by theoretically investigat- ing the impact of quantitative investing, a relatively underexplored form of algorithmic trading, on price efficiency. 7 The rest of the paper is structured as follows. Section 2 presents and solves the baseline model. Section 3 extends the model to two quant firms. Section 4 concludes. Appendix A contains the proofs, and Appendix B extends the model to allow for partial strategic myopia. 2. Model We consider a Kyle-type mo

Model We consider a Kyle-type model in which quantitative and discretionary traders coexist. The model allows us to study their interaction and examine how the increase in quant participation affects price efficiency. 2.1. Setup. Three groups of participants trade a risky security: (i) market makers, (ii) noise traders, and (iii) two investment firms (firmsA and B). The liquidation value v of the security is given by v = v1 + v2, where v1 and v2 are independent and identically dis- tributed normal variables with mean zero and varianceΣ0. Each investment firm consists of a discretionary department D and a quant department Q. The discretionary depart- ment observes v1 and trades akin to the rational Kyle (1985) insider. The quant depart- ment additionally observes a noisy signalθ2 = v2 + ϵ with ϵ ∼ N (0, σ2 ϵ ) about the s

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: The discretionary depart- ment observes v1 and trades akin to the rational Kyle (1985) insider. The quant depart- ment additionally observes a noisy signalθ2 = v2 + ϵ with ϵ ∼ N (0, σ2 ϵ ) about the second component of the liquidation value. We first investigate the case in which firm A is fully discretionary, while firm B has a discretionary department D and a quant department Q. We solve the general model, where both firms have both departments, in Section 3. The model has three dates, t ∈ {0, 1, 2}, as shown in Figure 1. At t = 0 , nature determines which department of firm B participates in the market. The quant department is active with probability γ, and the discretionary department with probability 1 − γ, where 0 ⩽ γ ⩽ 1 measures the level of quantitative investing. The realisation of department Q’s or departmentD’s presence is only observed by firmB. [Figure 1 here] Trading occurs att = 1. Let p and w denote the price and aggregate order flow att = 1, respectively. We begin by

We begin by assuming that traders rationally conjecture the price function as p = λ · ω. Given this conjecture and the opponent’s demandxj, each trader i ∈ {A, D, Q} 8 endowed with an information setϕi submits a market order of sizexi to maximize expected trading profits, E [(v − p(ϕi, xi, xj))xi|ϕi, xi] . (1) For the discretionary departments, the information set is ϕA = ϕD = v1. For the quant department, it is ϕQ = {v1, θ2}. The additional signal θ2 captures the greater information processing capacity of the quant department. The quality of the additional signal is q = Σ0/(Σ0 + σ2 ϵ ). Although the quant department processes more information, it is strategically weaker than the discretionary department. To capture this, we assume that the quant department of firm B ignores the fact that its trading

text: t department processes more information, it is strategically weaker than the discretionary department. To capture this, we assume that the quant department of firm B ignores the fact that its trading can reduce the aggressiveness of firmA. While the actual demand of firmA is xA = E(v | ϕA) 2λ − E(xB | ϕA) 2 , (2) the quant department incorrectly perceives it as xA = E(v|ϕA) 2λ . (3) This reflects the strategic weakness of the quant department, as it ignores its impact on the opponent’s behavior. Noise traders trade according to their liquidity or hedging needs exogenous to the model. We assume they demand u ∼ N(0, Σ0) units for simplicity. Market makers ob- serve the aggregate order flow (sum of all traders’ demands) as ω = xA + γ · xQ + (1 − γ) · xD + u. (4) They are risk neutral and competitive. Therefore, the equilibrium pricing rule satisfies weak market efficiency and allows them to break even in expectation, i.e.,p = E[v|ω]. 2.2. Discussion of assumptions. Most of the assumptions

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: etitive. Therefore, the equilibrium pricing rule satisfies weak market efficiency and allows them to break even in expectation, i.e.,p = E[v|ω]. 2.2. Discussion of assumptions. Most of the assumptions in our setup are standard and follow the tradition of Kyle (1985). The model introduces two non-standard assumptions to capture key differences between quants and discretionary investors. 9 First, we assume that quants have access to an additional noisy signal θ2 about a sec- ond component of the asset’s value. This reflects their superior ability to process richer or higher-dimensional data sources. Industry reports document the growing reliance of quantitative funds on non-traditional data sources (e.g., news feeds, social media, com- munications metadata, satellite imagery, and geo-spatial data) to gain an informational advantage.2 A similar structure appears in Easley, O’Hara and Yang (2013), who model hedge funds as having access to additional information and unique investment opport

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: data) to gain an informational advantage.2 A similar structure appears in Easley, O’Hara and Yang (2013), who model hedge funds as having access to additional information and unique investment opportu- nities compared to traditional investors. Second, we assume that quants are strategically myopic. They optimise trades based on their additional signals but fail to internalise how their actions affect discretionary investors. This captures the idea that many quantitative strategies are designed to ex- ploit data patterns without accounting for second-order strategic feedback. Abis (2022) also argues that humans are more flexible in adapting to changing market conditions, whereas traditional rule-based programming limits the adaptability of quantitative strate- gies. While we push strategic myopia to the extreme in the baseline model for clarity, we show in the extended model in Appendix B that as long as quants remain less strategic than discretionary investors, our key results continu

text: myopia to the extreme in the baseline model for clarity, we show in the extended model in Appendix B that as long as quants remain less strategic than discretionary investors, our key results continue to hold. 2.3. Equilibrium. The equilibrium concept is a perfect Bayesian Nash equilibrium, de- fined as follows: (i) Each traderi chooses a trading strategyxi(ϕi, E(xj)) that maximises expected trad- ing profit, given their information setϕi and beliefs about the opponent’s demand E(xj); (ii) The market maker chooses a pricing strategyp(ω) that breaks even in expectation; (iii) Traders and the market maker use Bayes’ theorem to form beliefs. To derive the equilibrium, we start with the optimisation problem defined in Eq. (1) and derive the first-order conditions. Solving the optimisation problem of firm A yields its best response as xA = αAv1, where 2 See, for example, Deloitte’s InFocus report on ’Alternative data adoption in investing and finance’. 10 αA = 1 2λ − γαQ 2 − (1 − γ)αD 2 .

When quantitative investing level γ increases, the impact on overall trading ag- gressiveness τ depends on three effects: (i) Processing capacity effect (positive): As firm B relies more on its quant department, it trades more aggressively on the additional signal (θ2) due to improved information pro- cessing capacity: ∂eβB ∂γ = 3q 2 . (13) (ii) Strategic myopia effect (negative): The quant department of firm B is less

(15) An increase in the level of quantitative investing (γ) increases both the information pro- cessing capacity and the strategic inflexibility of firm B. Higher processing capacity im- proves access to the additional signalθ2, giving firmB a stronger informational advantage in estimating v2. In this extended model, firm A consists of a quant department Q1 and a discretionary department D1, de- parting from the baseline where it was fully discretionary. Similarly, firm B has a quant department Q2 and di

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: ed model, firm A consists of a quant department Q1 and a discretionary department D1, de- parting from the baseline where it was fully discretionary. Similarly, firm B has a quant department Q2 and discretionary department D2. At t = 0, nature determines which de- partment is active in each firm. The probability that firm A’s quant department is active is denoted by γ1 ∈ [0, 1] and the corresponding probability for firmB is γ2 ∈ [0, 1]. All traders know that the price function att = 1 takes the form ofp = λ′ω. The demands of D1, Q1, D2 and Q2 are denoted byxD1, xQ1, xD2, and xQ2, respectively. Since firmA is no longer fully discretionary, both firmA’s and firmB’s ex-ante demand is a weighted sum of demands from their respective quant and discretionary departments: xA = γ1xQ1 + (1 − γ1)xD1, (17) xB = γ2xQ2 + (1 − γ2)xD2. When both firms have quant departments, the impact of quantitative investing level γ2 of firm B on overall trading aggressivenessτ ′ depends on four effects: (i) Processing capacity effect (positive): As firm B relies more on its quant department, it trades more aggressively on the additional signal (θ2) due to improved information pro- cessing capacity: ∂eβ ′ B ∂γ2 = 3q(2 − γ1) 4 . (32) 17 (ii) Strategic myopia effect (negative): The quant department of firm B is less flexible and ignores its strategic impact, which leads to less aggressive trading onv1: ∂eα ′ B ∂γ2 = − 3γ1 + 3 (γ1 + γ2 − γ1γ2 + 3)2 . An increase in the quantitative investing levelγ2 leads to: (i) firm B trading more aggressively onθ2, as it benefits from improved capacity to process this signal; (ii) firm B trading less aggressively on v1, due to greater strategic inflexibility and a higher likelihood of overestimating the opponent’s aggressiveness; and (iii) firm A trading more aggressively on v1, as its discretionary department internalises firm B’s reduced trading aggressiveness. As a result, firm A’s quant department trades less aggressively on θ2. We refer to this reduction in trading aggres- siveness by firm A as the competition effect. Corollary 2 analytically describes how these four effects on trading aggressiveness vary withq and γ2. Corollary 2. When both firms have quant departments, the magnitude of the processing capacity effect increases in signal qualityq at a faster rate than the magnitude of the competition effect. Growth in quantitative investing by both firms.In the previous subsection, we ex- amined how an increase in firmB’s quantitative investing level affects trading aggressive- ness and price efficiency, holding firmA’s level fixed. We now investigate what happens when both firms increase their quantitative investing levels. To answer this, we analyse the second-order cross-partial derivatives of eα,′ A (the normalised trading aggressiveness of firm A on signal v1) and the price efficiency with respect to γ1 and γ2. The results are summarised in the following corollary and illustrated in Figure 5. Corollary 3. (i) The internalising effect of an increase in firmB’s quantitative investing levelγ2 decreases as firm A’s quantitative investing levelγ1 increases. (ii) The marginal effect of γ2 on price efficiency decreases asγ1 increases. Recall from the previous analysis that as firm B’s quantitative investing level γ2 in- creases, firm A’s discretionary department res

text: arginal effect of γ2 on price efficiency decreases asγ1 increases. Recall from the previous analysis that as firm B’s quantitative investing level γ2 in- creases, firm A’s discretionary department responds by trading more aggressively onv1 (an internalising effect), i.e., ∂eα,′ A/∂γ2 > 0. However, as stated in part (i) of Corollary 3, this internalising effect decreases as γ1 increases, i.e., ∂2eα,′ A/∂γ2, ∂γ1 < 0 (see Panel A of Figure 5). 20 [Figure 5 here] Part (ii) of Corollary 3 further implies that the price efficiency impact of increasing firm B’s quantitative investing levelγ2 is more likely to be negative when firm A’s levelγ1 is high (see Panel B of Figure 5). In this case, the benefits of firmB’s improved information processing are increasingly offset by its reduced strategic responsiveness and by weaker internalisation from firm A. As a result, an increase in γ2 is more likely to reduce over- all trading aggressiveness and harm price efficiency when firmA’s quantitative inv

text: sponsiveness and by weaker internalisation from firm A. As a result, an increase in γ2 is more likely to reduce over- all trading aggressiveness and harm price efficiency when firmA’s quantitative investing level is high. To further illustrate the negative impact of increasing firm B’s quantitative investing level γ2 on price efficiency when firm A is already fully quantitative, Figure 6 plots price efficiency against γ2 for γ1 = 1 and three different levels of signal quality:q = 0.1, q = 0.5, and q = 0.9. The downward-sloping curves indicate that price efficiency is always harmed by increases in firmB’s quantitative investing levelγ2 when the firm A is fully quant. [Figure 6 here] One may interpret firm A’s quantitative investing level as a proxy for the overall mar- ket’s reliance on quantitative strategies, excluding firmB. When this market-level quant intensity is already high, regulators and practitioners should closely monitor further in- creases in quantitative investing by indi

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: ntitative strategies, excluding firmB. When this market-level quant intensity is already high, regulators and practitioners should closely monitor further in- creases in quantitative investing by individual firms. The analytical results and numerical examples in this section suggest that policy interventions aimed at curbing excessive quan- titative trading could enhance price efficiency in markets where such activity is already quite high. 4. Conclusion We theoretically examine the strategic interaction between quantitative and discre- tionary investors and its impact on market efficiency. From this interaction, we isolate four key effects. First, the processing capacity effect reflects quants’ superior ability to ex- tract and process information, leading them to trade more aggressively on the additional signal they observe. Second, the strategic myopia effectcaptures quants’ limited awareness 21 of how their trades influence other traders, causing them to trade less aggressively on

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: on the additional signal they observe. Second, the strategic myopia effectcaptures quants’ limited awareness 21 of how their trades influence other traders, causing them to trade less aggressively on the public signal. Third, discretionary investors respond strategically by increasing their trading aggressiveness on the public signal, which we term theinternalising effect. Finally, when multiple quants trade on similar signals, their informational advantage is diluted through a competition effect, leading them to trade less aggressively on the additional sig- nal. Together, these forces create a non-monotonic relationship between quantitative par- ticipation and market efficiency. We find that better processing capacity of quants can improve price efficiency, but only when the quality of their private signals is sufficiently strong or their strategic awareness is sufficiently high. In contrast, when quants dominate trading but fail to internalise their market impact, they may crowd int

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: heir private signals is sufficiently strong or their strategic awareness is sufficiently high. In contrast, when quants dominate trading but fail to internalise their market impact, they may crowd into similar positions, trigger feedback loops, and reduce price informativeness. These findings provide a theoretical foundation for empirical pat- terns observed during episodes such as the Quant Meltdown (e.g., Khandani and Lo 2011; Stein 2009), the May 2010 Flash Crash (e.g., Menkveld and Yueshen 2019), and recent fire- sale spillovers (e.g., Falato et al. 2021), where herding among algorithmic traders led to market instability. From a policy standpoint, the model suggests that interventions should be sensitive to both the overall level of quantitative investing and the diversity of trading strategies. For example, promoting diversity in how trading signals are processed through incentives for differentiated models, open access to alternative data sources, or regulatory sandbox pro- grams

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: trategies. For example, promoting diversity in how trading signals are processed through incentives for differentiated models, open access to alternative data sources, or regulatory sandbox pro- grams that support the safe testing of novel algorithmic strategies can help reduce herding. Complementing this, circuit breakers that adjust dynamically based on order flow compo- sition, such as triggering earlier when trading is dominated by algorithms, can slow down destabilizing feedback loops. Transparency around models can further support regula- tors in assessing the risk of coordination failures in algorithmic trading. These measures help mitigate the systemic risks that arise when traders with limited strategic awareness respond simultaneously to similar signals. For asset managers and market participants, our analysis cautions against over-reliance on signal-based models without accounting for their strategic impact on other investors. 22 As quantitative strategies become commoditise

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: rket participants, our analysis cautions against over-reliance on signal-based models without accounting for their strategic impact on other investors. 22 As quantitative strategies become commoditised, their relative advantage can erode not only due to competition, but also due to second-order effects from market instability. Ul- timately, our model shows price efficiency is not just about processing more data, but also about understanding how others react to it. 23 References Abis, S. Hence, ∂PEboth quants/∂γ2 ∂γ1 < 0 for all combinations of γ1, γ2, and q. ■ 42 Appendix B: Partially myopic quant In the baseline model, firmA is fully discretionary, and firmB has a quant department that is assumed to have complete strategic myopia. That is, it entirely ignores the impact of its trading on firmA’s behavior. In this appendix, we relax this assumption by allowing for partial strategic responsiveness. Specifically, for proportionh of the quant departmentQ’s orders, Q incorrectly ignores that the fully discretionary firmA reacts to the corresponding price impact by reducing trading aggressiveness, where0 < h ≤ 1. Therefore, h captures the degree of strategic inflexibility or myopia within the quant department, ranging from full strategic awareness whenh = 0 to complete strategic myopia whenh = 1. Importantly, the strategic myopia effect,∂eα∗ B/∂γ, and the internalising effect,∂eα∗ A/∂γ, are both indepen- dent of signal qualityq and vanish as the degree of strategic responsivenessh approaches 44 zero. In contrast, the processing capacity effect,∂eβ∗ B/∂γ, is independent of h and remains constant regardless of the degree of strategic responsiveness. Corollary B1. When the quant department of firmB ignores its price impact with probabilityh: (i) The magnitude of the processing capacity effect increases with signal qualityq. (ii) The magnitude of the strategic myopia effect decreases with the quantitative investing level γ at a faster rate than the internalising effect. (iii) The magnitude of the strategic myopia effect increases withh at a faster rate than the inter- nalising effect. Corollary B1 describes how the three main effects

text: e than the internalising effect. (iii) The magnitude of the strategic myopia effect increases withh at a faster rate than the inter- nalising effect. Corollary B1 describes how the three main effects (processing capacity, strategic my- opia, and internalising effects) change with signal quality q, quantitative investing level γ, and the degree of strategic myopia h. Two key insights follow. First, the relationships of these effects with respect to q and γ remain qualitatively consistent with Corollary 1. Second, increasing h amplifies the strategic myopia effect more than the internalising ef- fect. The intuition is that a more myopic quant department (higherh) contributes more to strategic inflexibility, causing a sharper decline in firmB’s trading aggressiveness onv1. As a result, the overall impact of increasingγ on trading aggressiveness is more likely to be positive whenq is high orh is low (processing capacity dominates), and more likely to be negative when q is low or h is high

Proposition B3. When the quant department of firm B ignores its price impact with probability h, the effect of an increase in quantitative investing levelγ on price efficiency depends on the ratio of

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: Proposition B3. When the quant department of firm B ignores its price impact with probability h, the effect of an increase in quantitative investing levelγ on price efficiency depends on the ratio of signal quality q to the degree of strategic myopiah: (i) When 0 < q/h ≤ a1, price efficiency decreases with the level of quantitative investingγ. (ii) When a1 < q/h < a 2, price efficiency decreases with the level of quantitative investingγ if and only if γ < ¯γ∗. (iii) When q/h ≥ a2, price efficiency increases with the level of quantitative investingγ. When the ratio q/h is low (0 < q/h ≤ a1), indicating either low signal quality or high strategic myopia, price efficiency decreases with increased quantitative investing (see Fig- ure B2). This is because the strategic myopia effect dominates when q is low or h is high (see Panels A and F of Figure B1). When q/h is high (q/h ≥ a2), suggesting either high signal quality or low strategic myopia, price efficiency increases with quantitative in

paper_title: QUANTS AND MARKET EFFICIENCY
text: hen q is low or h is high (see Panels A and F of Figure B1). When q/h is high (q/h ≥ a2), suggesting either high signal quality or low strategic myopia, price efficiency increases with quantitative invest- ing. In this case, the processing capacity effect dominates (Panels C and D of Figure B1). For moderate values of q/h (a1 < q/h < a 2), price efficiency is non-monotonic in γ. It initially decreases when γ < ¯γ∗ but increases γ exceeds ¯γ∗, as in the baseline model.
